coredata.dc:description: Pineapple leaf waste, a byproduct of agricultural processes, was used as a novel raw material to synthesize carbon dots (CDs) through a simple hydrothermal method. The CDs were subsequently incorporated into pineapple stem starch (PSS)-based active food packaging films. The characterization of the CDs and PSS-CDs films was conducted using various techniques, including UV-light spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, and transmission electron microscopy. The results revealed that the CDs measured 2.36 ± 0.33 nm and exhibited antioxidant and antibacterial activities. The addition of the CDs led to notable enhancements in both mechanical strength and UV-barrier properties. Thus, PSS-CDs packaging film was successfully prepared, with the incorporation of CDs enhancing the antioxidant and antimicrobial properties of the film, thereby extending the shelf-life of fresh pork.

head.ce:title: Pineapple stem starch-based films incorporated with pineapple leaf carbon dots as functional filler for active food packaging applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Pineapple leaf waste, a byproduct of agricultural processes, was used as a novel raw material to synthesize carbon dots (CDs) through a simple hydrothermal method. The CDs were subsequently incorporated into pineapple stem starch (PSS)-based active food packaging films. The characterization of the CDs and PSS-CDs films was conducted using various techniques, including UV-light spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, and transmission electron microscopy. The results revealed that the CDs measured 2.36 ± 0.33 nm and exhibited antioxidant and antibacterial activities. The addition of the CDs led to notable enhancements in both mechanical strength and UV-barrier properties. Thus, PSS-CDs packaging film was successfully prepared, with the incorporation of CDs enhancing the antioxidant and antimicrobial properties of the film, thereby extending the shelf-life of fresh pork.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Active packaging has been designed to extend the shelf-life of foods and enhance food safety and quality over time. Various methods have been explored for food preservation through the development of active films [1–3]. There is a growing interest in active packaging with antimicrobial and UV-barrier properties within the food packaging sector. The demand for antimicrobial packaging films is on the rise due to their ability to shield food from microbial contamination and prevent spoilage [4]. During the shelf-life of products, exposure to UV radiation leads to the formation of oxidative free radicals, which accelerate the deterioration of food and result in rancidity, off-flavors, discoloration, and decreased nutritional value [5,6]. The main food components sensitive to UV radiation, normally known as photosensitizers, include vitamins and typical food colorants such as oils, fats, chlorophylls, and myoglobin [7,8]. Therefore, to ensure food safety, it is crucial to employ packaging technologies with appropriate antibacterial and UV-barrier properties agents that play an essential role in mitigating oxidation and contamination of food, thereby extending shelf-life and preserving its safety and quality standards [9,10]. Biopolymers, when combined with functional nanomaterials, have garnered significant attention for their potential to replace non-degradable plastic packaging materials [11–13].

Recently, various nanomaterials from different sources have been applied to develop active films that play an active role in food preservation. Carbon dots (CDs), emerging as a novel class of nanomaterials, have gained attention across various fields for their economic abundance and multifunctional properties [13,14]. A carbon dot (CD) is a zero-dimensional nanomaterial composed of carbon atoms arranged in a spherical structure, typically measuring <10 nm in size. Due to their small size, these CDs exhibit unique photoluminescent properties that differ from other carbon-based nanomaterials [15,16]. Moreover, CDs possess favorable characteristics, such as water solubility, biocompatibility, and low toxicity, enhancing their suitability for various applications [17,18]. Additionally, their controllable surface functional groups and size enable CDs to exhibit strong adsorption interactions with biological surfaces [19].

CDs have a complex chemical structure, comprising a carbon core adorned with surface functional groups [20]. The synthesis method and carbon source used during production significantly influence the core structure of CDs. Conversely, the surface functional groups originate from the carbon source and play a crucial role in determining various properties of CDs. Generally, surface groups found in CDs include hydroxyl, amino, and carboxyl groups, which significantly influence their surface characteristics [21,22]. Apart from their biological applications, CDs possess strong antioxidant and antimicrobial activities [23–26] due to their exclusive physicochemical properties, such as structural defects, surface charge, small size, and functional groups [27–29]. Thus, there has been a growing demand for CDs in food packaging, as they are regarded as active materials capable of forming antioxidant or antimicrobial agents for use in food packaging [30].

Currently, an efficient method for synthesizing CDs using a simple carbon source has been developed, which has found widespread application across numerous research fields due to its cost-effectiveness and sustainability. Various carbon source materials have been used to manufacture CDs, with their properties depending on the raw materials [31]. Biomass-derived carbon dots synthesized from biological waste conform to the principles of ecological environmental protection and the requirements of green chemistry, which show great application potential in practice [32,33]. Pineapple production stands as one of the most important agricultural sectors globally, generating various residues including peels, crowns, stems, and leaves. Typically, the latter three residues are left in fields for farmers to manage before commencing a new crop cultivation cycle [34]. Thailand is currently the world's largest pineapple producer with about 240,000 acres of cultivation area. After harvesting, a large amount of pineapple leaf waste remains causing various problems for farmers to deal with [35]. Agricultural waste, discarded in substantial quantities worldwide, presents an opportunity as a raw material for synthesizing CDs, thus transforming it into a sustainable and high-value-added functional material. Notably, pineapple leaves have emerged as a promising source of CDs, often obtained as a by-product of agricultural processes. The total carbohydrate content of raw pineapple leaf is 34.57 ± 2.94 %, making it an excellent carbon source for CD synthesis [36]. Moreover, pineapple leaf also contains free-associated phenolic compounds that provide functional properties along with strong antioxidant activity [37]. Therefore, synthesizing CDs from pineapple leaves and applying them to the production of functional packaging films holds the potential to utilize agricultural by-products and reduce carbon emissions.

The utilization of functional films derived from biopolymers has garnered considerable attention in the production of active packaging films, particularly within the area of food packaging and preservation. This expression of interest can be attributed to their biodegradability, biocompatibility, non-toxicity, eco-friendliness, and sustainability [38–40]. Notably, biopolymer-based food packaging films exhibit excellent biocompatibility and can serve various functions, such as providing antimicrobial and antioxidant properties, making them promising alternatives to plastic packaging films. Recently, pineapple stem starch (PSS) has emerged as a promising material for a variety of applications. This is because the PSS has a relatively high amylose content and it is abundant in pineapple field waste, particularly in Thailand [41,42]. PSS film exhibits desirable properties, such as high water resistance, low water absorption, and good mechanical strength, while remaining readily biodegradable [43]. Therefore, PSS has been selected for use as a biopolymer-based material in active packaging preparation.

The rationale for developing a pineapple stem starch (PSS) film incorporated with carbon dots (CDs) derived from pineapple leaf waste stems from several key factors. First, there is an increasing demand for sustainable, biodegradable packaging solutions that can also provide active functions such as antimicrobial and antioxidant properties. PSS, with its high amylose content and availability as an agricultural waste product, presents an excellent base material for such packaging. Second, while CDs have shown promise in various applications due to their unique properties, their potential in food packaging, particularly when derived from agricultural waste, remains largely unexplored. By utilizing pineapple leaf waste for CD synthesis, this study aims to create a closed-loop system that transforms agricultural by-products into high-value materials. Furthermore, the combination of PSS and CDs addresses multiple challenges simultaneously: it provides a use for agricultural waste, creates a biodegradable packaging material, and enhances food preservation through improved mechanical strength, UV-barrier properties, and antimicrobial/antioxidant activities. This approach not only aligns with principles of sustainable development and circular economy but also fills a gap in current research by exploring the synergistic effects of PSS and pineapple leaf-derived CDs in active food packaging applications. The development of this novel PSS-CDs film thus represents a significant step towards more sustainable and functional food packaging solutions.

To our current understanding, there exists a dearth of research on the synthesis of CDs derived from pineapple leaves. Therefore, this study aims to synthesize CDs from pineapple leaves and introduce them as functional additives in PSS-based active packaging films for the first time. The CDs and CDs-incorporated PSS films underwent characterization, and the effect of CDs on the physicochemical and functional properties of the films was investigated to develop active food packaging capable of enhancing the safety and shelf-life of fresh pork during chilled storage.

Pineapple leaves and stems (agricultural waste) were collected from pineapple fields in Phitsanulok, Thailand. The leaves and stem were prepared as the starting materials for pineapple leaf carbon dot (CDs) synthesis and pineapple stem starch (PSS) following the method of Kengkhetkit [44] and Nakthong [41], respectively. Glycerol and Citric acid were purchased from QReC (New Zealand). A commercial wrap film (PVC) was purchased from a local supermarket in Nakhon Ratchasima, Thailand. All the other chemicals were of the analytical grade.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of pineapple leaf carbon dots (CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: To optimize the conditions for synthesizing CDs, a simple hydrothermal method at 200 °C was utilized to produce CDs from pineapple leaf waste. The factors studied are shown in Table 1 . Pineapple leaf waste (0.8–4.0 g) was crushed with about 70 mL of each solution (70 mL of deionized water (DI water) and ethanol solution (35 mL of ethanol+35 mL of DI water)) then the residue was removed using a filter cloth. The liquid fraction was transferred to a 100 mL of poly(propylene) inner-equipped stainless steel autoclave, and 0–3 mL of NH3 solution was added in the case of DI water and heated at a constant temperature of 200 °C for 12–16 h. To increase the functional group of the CDs, 500–2000 μL of Ethylenediamine (EDA) or 4 g of urea was added. After the hydrothermal reaction, the autoclave was allowed to cool naturally to room temperature. The obtained yellowish-brown crude product was filtered using a membrane with a pore size of 0.2 μm to remove the large aggregated particles and impurities. The supernatant was transferred to a glass vial and stored for further characterization.

The pineapple stem starch (PSS) was prepared following the method described by Nakthong, with slight modifications [41]. The peeled and cut pineapple stem was stirred in water with a weight ratio of pineapple stem to water of 1:1.5 for 20 min. The fibrous materials were removed with a filter cloth. The liquid fraction was decanted from the filtrate fraction, which contained starch. Water was added to the starch fraction to clean and remove water-soluble materials. The PSS was separated using centrifugation for 20 min. The process was repeated several times until the liquid fraction was clear. The PSS paste was dried at 60 °C for 48 h. Finally, the dried starch was sieved through a test sieve of 63 μm diameter.

The PSS-CDs films were prepared by solution casting method. Firstly, 6%w/v PSS solution was prepared in distilled water at 80 °C for 1 h. Glycerol was added into PSS solution with 1.5%w/v and stirred for 30 min, then 1%w/v citric acid was added into PSS solution and further stirred for 30 min. The PSS solution was cooled to 80 °C, CDs were then added at 1, 3 and 6 % w/w (weight of polymer) into PSS solutions. These mixtures were stirred on a magnetic stirrer for 1 h to obtain homogenous solutions. The solutions were cooled to room temperature and 10 mL of each PSS solution was poured into a mold (10 cm diameter) and dried at room temperature for about 48 h. The dried PSS-CDs films were peeled off and stored in a desiccator. The films obtained at 0, 1, 3 and 6%w/w of CDs were designated as PSS, PSS-CDs1%, PSS-CDs3% and PSS-CDs6%, respectively.

Ultraviolet-visible spectroscopy (UV-VIS; UV-2450, Shimadzu), Fluorescence spectroscopy (RF-5301PC Spectrofluorophotometer, Shimadzu), Attenuated total reflectance-Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR; Spectrum100, Perkin Elmer Inc.), Transmission electron microscopy (FE-TEM; JEOL: JEM-3100F), were applied to study the properties of CDs. The light absorbance of the CDs solutions was provided in the wavelength range of 200–600 nm using UV-VIS spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence properties of CDs was investigated using a fluorescence spectrophotometer to determine the emission wavelength at an excitation wavelength range of 380–500 nm. The chemical structures of the CDs were analyzed using the ATR-FTIR in the range of 4000–650 cm−1. The morphology of the CDs was observed using an FE-TEM and the size and shape of the CDs determined. The CDs samples were prepared on a holey carbon copper grid. Firstly, the CDs solution was sonicated for 10 min, then the copper grid was immersed into the CDs solution for 5 min. After that, the copper grid was placed with the carbon side facing up on the filter paper and kept in a desiccator overnight.

The antimicrobial activity of CDs was tested against both Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria as S. aureus and E. coli, respectively, using the Disc diffusion method. Paper discs coated with 50 mg/mL of penomycin, distilled water and CDs solution were used as the positive control, negative control and CDs sample, respectively. The five CDs samples were placed on a Petri dish containing Nutrient Agar (Merck, Germany). Then, the Petri dishes were incubated at 37 °C for 24 h, and the diameter of the inhibition zone surrounding each CDs sample was determined in millimeters using a digital micrometer.

The appearance of the films was observed under normal daylight and UV light. The thickness of the film was measured at five different zones of each film sample using a hand-held digital thickness gauge (SMD-565 J, Japan). The average film thickness was calculated.
2)
Functional group analysis

The functional groups of the film samples were examined by ATR-FTIR at 4000–650 cm−1 by using a Spectrum100 (Perkin Elmer Inc.).
3)
Film transparency and UV-VIS barrier property

Each film sample was cut into pieces of 4 cm × 4 cm and placed in a sample holder. Then, % transmittance and absorbance at 200–800 nm was recorded using a UV-VIS spectrometer (UV-2450, Shimadzu). The % transmittance at wavelength 280 nm and 660 nm were recorded as T280 and T660, respectively.
4)
Mechanical performance test

Each film sample was cut into 5 rectangular pieces of 8 cm × 1 cm and placed at 53 % RH and 25 ± 5 °C for 24 h. The thickness was measured using a hand-held digital thickness gauge (SMD-565 J, Japan) at 5 randomly selected points on each segment. Mechanical properties, including tensile strength and % elongation at break were measured using a Universal tensile testing machine at a crosshead speed of 50 mm/min (model LRX Plus) according to the ASTM D882–91 (ASTM, 1992). The results were presented as the average values derived from the measurements.
5)
Water contact angle

Water contact angles of the film sample were measured by dropping 5 μL of deionized water with a micro syringe onto the film surface using a contact angle meter (OCA25, Dataphysics) at 25 °C. Contact angles were observed within 5 s, and the average of 3 measurements was calculated.
6)
Water vapor transmission rate (WVP)

The films (4 cm × 4 cm) were placed on top of individual vials (20 mL; 2 cm diameter) containing 3.0 g of CaCl2 and sealed with parafilm. The vials were placed in a desiccator containing 50 mL of water at room temperature (25 °C) such that one side was at 75 % relative humidity (RH2) and the other side at 0 % (RH1). The weight of the vials was recorded every 2 h for 12 h. The changes in weight over time were plotted to determine the WVP. The slope of the linear graph represented the steady state amount of water vapor diffusing through the film, with three films used for each sample. The WVP was calculated by the slope of the graph using Eq. (1).
(1)
WVP =
S × d
A × P
RH 2 − RH 1
where, S is the slope of the linear graph illustrating the changes in weight over time, d is the thickness (mm), A is the area (mm2), and P is the saturation vapor pressure of water at room temperature (0.0313 atm) [45].
7)
Water solubility and water swelling

Each film sample was cut into 3 pieces of 2 cm × 2 cm and dried at 60 °C for 24 h. The dried films were weighed (Wdi) and then immersed in 50 mL of distilled water at room temperature for 24 h. At each testing time, the films were removed from the water and the excess water on the surface blotted out, and the swollen films were weighed (Ws). After immersion for 24 h, each film was removed from the water and dried at 60 °C for 24 h and reweighed (Wdf). The degree of water swelling (DS) and the water solubility (WS) of the films were calculated using Eqs. (2) and (3), respectively.
(2)
DS =
Ws − Wdi
Wdi
× 100
(3)
WS =
Wdi − Wdf
Wdi
× 100
where, Wdi, Wdf, and Ws are the dried weight of initial film (g), the dried weight of final film (g) and the weight of water saturated film, respectively.
8)
Film morphology

The film samples were broken into small pieces in liquid nitrogen. These small pieces samples were then affixed in metal stubs with double-sided adhesive carbon tape and coated with gold. The morphological structures of the films were then examined using a field emission scanning electron microscope (AURIGA, Carl Zeiss).
9)
Thermal stability

The thermal stability and decomposition temperature of films were characterized using a thermogravimetric analyzer (TGA, Rigaku, TG8120 Thermoplus, Japan) under a nitrogen environment. The samples were heated from 25 to 600 °C at a rate of 10 °C/min.
10)
Antioxidant activity

The antioxidant activity of the PSS-CDs films was assessed using the 2,2-Diphenel-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) method, with slight modifications, as described by Mojarrab [46]. Initially, the sample solutions were prepared using methanol as the solvent. Each PSS-CDs film was cut into small pieces (2 cm × 2 cm) and dissolved in 5 mL of methanol, followed by agitation for 1 h at 27 ± 3 °C. Subsequently, 0.2 mM of DPPH solution was added to an equal volume of all test samples. The mixtures were vigorously shaken and kept in darkness for 30 min, after which absorbance was measured at 517 nm against a blank. Butylated hydroxytoluene (BHT) was used as a standard reference. The DPPH radical scavenging activity was then calculated using Eq. (4). Inhibition concentration (IC50) was calculated using the %radical scavenging activity.
(4)
% Radical scavenging activity =
A C
−
A S
A C
× 100 %
where, Ac is the absorbance of the control and As is the absorbance of the sample.
11)
Application of PSS-CDs active films to pork preservation

A test on meat packaging was conducted to assess the practicality of the UV-blocking application of the PSS-CDs films. Fresh pork was purchased from a local supermarket in Nakhon Ratchasima, Thailand, and cut into 4 g. Fresh pork samples were kept in a Petri plate with the PSS-CDs films covering the top. The commercial wrap film (PVC) and fresh pork sample without any film were used for comparison. All samples were stored under UV-light radiation. Two tubes of UV lamp (F20T12, 20 W, Fluorescent UV Tube, GE lighting, Peru S.A.) were used as a UV source, with a distance of 15 cm between the pork samples and the lamp. All the samples were kept at 35 °C and observed for 3 h for color changes, which were measured using a Chroma meter (Konica Minolta CR-400, Tokyo, Japan) and compared to the initial values.

Additionally, all samples were stored in a cold refrigerator at 12 ± 2 °C for a duration of up to 6 days. After this storage period, the overall quality of the samples was assessed through physicochemical analyses. Furthermore, to gauge positive (good) and negative (bad) odor perceptions of fresh pork samples, questionnaires were administered. These questionnaires were completed by faculty members from Rajamangala University of Technology Isan, located in Nakhon Ratchasima, Thailand. A total of 20 respondents were involved in evaluating the pork odor, which included unwrapped pork, commercially film-wrapped pork, and pork wrapped with the PSS-CDs films, all stored at 12 ± 2 °C for 6 days. The intensity of detected odor was recorded on a 5-point scale: 0 = no odor, 1 = possible odor presence, 2 = very faint negative odor, 3 = moderate negative odor intensity, 4 = strong negative odor intensity, and 5 = very strong negative odor intensity. Subsequently, the average odor score and standard deviation per respondent were calculated [47].

Total volatile basic nitrogen (TVB-N) analysis was conducted on the samples using a modified AOAC method [48]. A Conway cup with an outer diameter of 10 cm and an inner diameter of 5 cm was employed in this study. The procedure began with the preparation of a sample mixture by combining a pork sample (4 ± 0.01 g) with 8 mL of 7%w/v trichloroacetic acid (TCA) solution. This mixture underwent vortexing for 10 min, followed by a 30 min rest period at room temperature. Subsequently, the mixture was filtered using No. 1 filter paper. In the Conway cup, 1 mL of the resulting filtrate was introduced into the outer chamber. Concurrently, 1 mL of the inner ring solution (1 % w/v boric acid containing bromocresol green and methyl red) was placed in the inner chamber. The Conway cup was then nearly closed with its lid. Following this, 1 mL of saturated K2CO3 solution was added to the outer chamber. The cup was then fully closed and gently rotated to facilitate the mixing of the outer chamber contents. The blank was prepared following an identical protocol, substituting 7 % TCA for the filtrate. Both sample and blank solutions were incubated at room temperature for 2 h. Then, titration was performed using 0.01 N HCl until a pink color developed. TVB-N quantification utilized the following Eq. (5):
(5)
TVB − N
content
mg
N / 100 g
=
V c
−
V b
× 14.007 × N × 100
W
where, Vc is the volume of HCl used in sample titration, Vb is volume of HCl used in blank titration, N is the normality of the HCl solution, W is the sample weight (g), 14.007 is the molecular weight of nitrogen. This measurement was carried out with five replicates.
12)
Statistical analysis

The experimental data underwent analysis of variance. Significant differences among the average values were determined using Duncan and Tukey Test with a confidence level of 95 %. Data were analyzed using Microsoft Excel 2021 and SPSS 29.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of pineapple leaf carbon dots (CDs)

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence is the main characteristic of CDs, which verifies the success of their synthesis. To confirm the optimal condition of CDs synthesis, a preliminary study was conducted by comparing the fluorescence spectrum intensity of the CDs suspension obtained under different synthesis conditions; the results are shown in Fig. 1 .

The highest fluorescence intensity of the CDs synthesized using NH3 solution was observed at 380 nm, consistent with the expected property of CDs, which typically exhibit peak emission intensity within 340–390 nm [49]. Conversely, the CDs synthesized using ethanol solution exhibited a maximum emission intensity at 400 nm, deviating from the expected range (Fig. 1(a)). Moreover, it was observed that after one week of storage, sedimentation occurred in the CDs suspension using ethanol as the solvent, accompanied by a decrease in luminosity. In contrast, the CDs suspension prepared using NH3 solution showed no sedimentation, and the luminosity remained consistent. Therefore, the NH3 solution was selected as the solvent for CDs synthesis.

The fluorescence intensity of the CDs increased with hydrothermal reaction time from 12 to 16 h, reaching a maximum at 16 h; however, when the reaction time increased to 18 h, the fluorescence intensity of CDs decreased (Fig. 1(b)). This is because the longer reaction time increases a complete reaction, but the excessive reaction time could increase ash or sediment in the final product leading to a decrease in emission intensity [50]. Therefore, a synthesis time of 16 h was selected for the CDs synthesis.

Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs increased with the amount of pineapple leaf waste from 0.8 to 2.4 g, reaching a maximum at 2.4 g; however, a further increase to 3.2 g led to a decrease in fluorescence intensity (Fig. 1(c)). This observation suggests that pineapple leaf is a carbon source for CDs synthesis, while higher amounts of pineapple leaf waste contribute to increased synthesized CDs, excessive amounts may result in an incomplete reaction.

The fluorescence spectra in Fig. 1(d) indicated the highest intensity when ethylenediamine (EDA) was added to the solution medium compared to urea addition or no addition. The superior effectiveness of EDA in introducing functional groups to the surface of CDs is attributed to its molecular structure, which includes two amine groups (−NH2). This facilitates efficient interaction with functional groups on the CDs surface, leading to effective functionalization. Further investigations revealed that the inclusion of EDA during CDs synthesis resulted in the formation of nitrogen-containing functional groups, influencing the light emission properties of CDs [51]. Moreover, the optimal amounts of EDA and NH3 added were determined to be 1000 μL and 2 mL, respectively (Fig. 1(e) and Fig. 1(f)).

Based on the fluorescence spectra (Fig. 1), it can be observed that the optimal conditions for synthesizing CDs from pineapple leaf waste using the hydrothermal method at 200 °C were 2.4 g of pineapple leaf waste in 70 mL of DI water with the addition of 2 mL of NH3 and 1000 μL of EDA. The UV–Vis spectrum and fluorescence spectra of the obtained CDs under these conditions are depicted in Fig. 2 .

The fabricated CDs solution exhibited a transparent yellowish-brown color under normal daylight, which shifted to a greenish-blue under UV light due to the quantum confinement effect (Fig. 2 (a)) [39,52]. This observed phenomenon aligns with previously reported findings on CDs synthesized from dwarf banana peels [53,54]. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the synthesized CDs (Fig. 2 (a)) showed two absorbance bands centered at 293 and 339 nm. The absorption peak at 293 nm was attributed to the π-π* transition of aromatic domains (CC bonds) within the carbonized core of the CDs, while the peak at 339 nm was ascribed to the n-π* transitions of CO and CN bonds from the functional groups that are on the surface and edges of CDs [21,22,55,56]. The fluorescence excitation spectra of CDs revealed a maximum intensity centered around 380 nm (Fig. 2 (b)). Accordingly, the detailed fluorescence emission spectra of CDs were obtained by varying the excitation wavelength from 380 to 460 nm in 20 nm increments. As observed in the fluorescence emission spectra, the fluorescence emission intensity of the CDs gradually increased up to an excitation wavelength of 380 nm, and then the intensities gradually decreased with a red shift. The maximum emission was observed at a wavelength of 440 nm with an excitation wavelength of 380 nm. The red shift of fluorescence spectra suggests that the emission of CDs could be manipulated by altering the excitation wavelength. The excitation-independent emission behavior of CDs might be attributed to variations in CD sizes and surface defects resulting from the presence of different functional groups on the surface and edges of the CDs [14,53,57,58].

FT-IR spectroscopy was employed to identify the functional groups within the CDs. Fig. 3 shows that the CDs exhibited a main characteristic absorption peak of O-H/N-H stretching vibration mode at 3373 cm−1. Additionally, a C − H stretching vibration peak was evident at 2931 cm−1, while the peak at 1640 cm−1 corresponded to CO stretching of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons. The O − H bending vibration peak was observed at 1366 cm−1, and peaks in the range of approximately 1071–1091 cm−1 corresponded to CO stretching. The small vibrational band at 925–883 cm−1 was attributed to the C-O/C-C stretching. Furthermore, the FT-IR spectrum revealed the C-N-C stretching and CN stretching peaks at 1461 cm−1 and 1255 cm−1, respectively, confirming the presence of functional groups from EDA addition [59–61]. The results indicated that the CDs contained sufficient carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amine groups.

The TEM images (Fig. 4 ) show that the CDs particles appear spherical. Increasing the synthesis time from 16 h (Fig. 4 (a)) to 24 h (Fig. 4 (b)) caused the particle size distribution of the CDs to became non-uniform, with some particles falling outside the desired range of smaller than 10 nm. This resulted in a decrease in the fluorescence of the CDs suspension (Fig. 1 (b)). This result can be attributed to the prolonged hydrothermal process under conditions of high temperature and pressure, which affects the structure of the CDs, rendering the surface more susceptible to cell wall breakdown and leading to an accumulation of ash or sludge [31]. In terms of the effect of adding or not adding of EDA, it was observed that the particle size of CDs with added EDA (Fig. 4 (c)) was smaller than that of CDs without EDA (Fig. 4 (a)). This indicates that EDA acts as a functional group enhancer, modifying the structure and functional groups of the CDs and consequently altering their fluorescence [18,31]. The size of CDs with addition of different amounts of NH3, 1 mL (Fig. 4 (c)) and 2 mL (Fig. 4 (d)) were analyzed. It was found that CDs with 2 mL of NH3 exhibited smaller particle sizes than those with 1 mL of NH3. The type and concentration of solvent play a crucial role in determining the size of the synthesized CDs, as they facilitate the removal of water (dehydration) and carbon formation (carbonization), with each solvent behaving differently [62]. Consequently, it can be concluded that the CDs synthesized under the optimal conditions (hydrothermal reaction time of 16 h, treatment with 1000 μL of EDA, with the addition of 2 mL of NH3) were spherical and well-dispersed with an average diameter of 2.36 ± 0.33 nm (Fig. 4 (d)). The size of the obtained CDs was lower than 10 nm, which is in the theoretical range for CDs, demonstrating the successful preparation of carbon dots through the carbonization of pineapple leaf waste.

Incorporating active compounds like antioxidants, antimicrobials, and UV-barrier agents into packaging films can enhance the quality and safety of food products. The CDs were utilized as an active compound and aimed to be incorporated into PSS film to provide antimicrobial protection for fresh pork. Microbial contamination is a significant factor contributing to food spoilage, making it crucial for CDs intended for food applications to exhibit antibacterial activity. The antimicrobial efficacy of CDs against common foodborne pathogens was assessed, and the results are presented in Fig. 5 .

The inhibition zone results indicated that the CDs were significantly effective against S. aureus (Gram-positive) and slightly effective against E. coli (Gram-negative), which correspond to the positive control. This result was consistent with the previous studies reported by [63]. The antibacterial effectiveness of CDs is influenced by various factors, such as functional groups, shape, particle size, and the specific type of bacteria. CDs can damage membranes, leading to bacterial mortality due to their small size and high surface-to-volume ratio. CDs may cover the cell surface, causing disruption, or release of free radicals that damage lipids, nucleotides, and proteins [64]. Some studies on the impact of CDs on S. aureus and E. coli have revealed that CDs adhere to the bacterial surface, resulting in surface roughening, which hinders nutrient entry and reproduction. Furthermore, CDs containing oxygen groups (CO and CO) are absorbed into the bacterial cell wall before penetrating the interior. Subsequently, CDs bind to bacterial DNA and RNA through non-covalent bonds, altering their secondary structure and affecting genetic processes. Additionally, the adsorbed CDs can damage the bacterial cell wall, leading to the leakage of cellular contents [65].

This study revealed that the CDs exhibited stronger antimicrobial activity against Gram-positive bacteria (S. aureus) compared to the Gram-negative bacteria (E. coli). These findings are consistent with those of Travlou [66], and could be attributed to differences in cell wall characteristics, with Gram-positive bacteria having thicker and more permeable cell walls than Gram-negative bacteria [67]. Thus, this result show that the CDs synthesized from pineapple leaf waste has the potential for use as an active agent for active food packaging applications.

Table 2 shows that the thickness of the PSS films incorporated with CDs at 0, 1, 3, and 6 % were not significantly different. In this study, adding CDs in the range of 1–6 % did not affect the thickness of the films. This could have been due to the relatively small amounts of added CDs. Typically, adding a solid component to films increases their thickness; however, the minimal amount of CDs did not produce a notable impact. The PSS-CDs films displayed a transparent yellowish-brown color under normal light, which changed to a greenish-blue color under UV light, resembling the color of CDs solution (Fig. 2 (a)). The intensity of film color corresponded directly to the amount of CDs incorporated. Additionally, these films demonstrated flexibility, allowing easy bending and folding into desired shapes without cracking.

FT-IR spectroscopy is utilized to identify chemical functionalities and categorize interactions of polymer chains in polymer blends or modified materials. The FT-IR spectra of CDs, PSS film, and PSS-CDs films with different CDs concentrations are presented in Fig. 6 .

All peaks observed in CDs and PSS films also appeared in the PSS-CDs films with slight chemical shift values. The peak at 3275–3373 cm−1 was attributed to OH stretching. The OH stretching peak of the PSS-CDs films was observed at a similar position to that of the PSS film. The absorption peaks at 2926–2927 cm−1, 1641–1575 cm−1, 1320–1329 cm−1, and 1150 cm−1 corresponded to CH stretching, CO stretching, OH bending, and CN stretching of the PSS-CDs films, respectively. Moreover, the addition of CDs did not significantly alter the spectra, indicating that there was no chemical reaction between CDs and the PSS film. CDs were effectively incorporated into the PSS film without altering the film structure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–vis barrier property and film transparency
ce:section.ce:para: Exposure to UV light can degrade food quality and reduce its shelf life through oxidation. UV light, particularly below 400 nm, significantly influences the quality, nutritional value, and biochemical reactions of food, including odor, flavor, and color [68,69]. Therefore, protection from UV light is an important characteristic of active food packaging [70,71]. The UV–Vis absorbance and transmittance of the PSS films with various CDs concentrations were thoroughly examined across the UV (200–400 nm) and visible (400–800 nm) ranges, as shown in Fig. 7 .

The PSS film displayed negligible light absorbance, appearing highly transparent in the visible region, with 88.55 % light transmittance at 660 nm (T660). However, the PSS film exhibited deficient UV-blocking properties. Incorporating CDs into the PSS films caused a slight decrease in transmittance for visible light at higher wavelengths (> 600 nm), while significant light blocking occurred in the low wavelength (400–600 nm) region. The UV absorption of the PSS film was significantly lower than the absorption variations among the PSS films with different CDs concentrations, suggesting that the enhanced UV blocking can be attributed to the incorporation of CDs into the PSS matrix. Interestingly, with 3 % and 6 % w/w CDs incorporation, >85 % of light transmission occurred in the visible region, while over 95 % UV blocking was achieved (Fig. 7). Although the UV-blocking mechanism of CDs is not fully understood, this capability may be attributed to the presence of various functional groups on the surface of CDs. Additionally, altered functional groups provide electron transition of n-π* or π-π* orbitals. Sufficient conjugated structures can generate photostability and UV-light-blocking activity in the film [72]. Besides, the UV-blocking properties of CDs have been demonstrated in diverse packaging materials, including polyvinyl alcohol [73,74], pectin/gelatin [75], cellulose nanofibers [38], and chitosan [45]. These results indicate that the PSS-CDs films, prepared from agricultural waste, can be considered as transparent films with UV barrier properties without the need for semiconducting nanomaterials. This UV-blocking efficacy is crucial for protecting packaged food from UV-induced photodegradation [76].

The resistance to mechanical stress and flexibility, which are represented by tensile strength and elongation, respectively, are crucial considerations for packaging films because they directly influence product preservation during handling, transportation, and marketing [70]. According to Table 3 , the PSS films with CDs (PSS-CDs1–3%) showed higher tensile strength compared to films without CDs (PSS), although their flexibility was reduced. Additionally, in comparison to findings from other studies, both the tensile strength and elongation values of the PSS-CDs films surpassed those of active films made from neat starch film [77]. Notably, among the PSS-CDs films, PSS-CDs3% displayed the most favorable mechanical properties. Specifically, the tensile strength of the PSS-CDs3% film was approximately 2 times higher than that of the PSS film, while the elongation at break was only lower by <1.5 times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water contact angle and water vapor transmission rate (WVTR)
ce:section.ce:para: Moisture penetration in packaging films can significantly impact product quality and shelf-life. To assess the effect of CDs on the barrier properties of the PSS film, water vapor transmission rate (WVTR) and hydrophobicity of their surfaces were investigated using contact angle analysis, the results are shown in Table 4 .

Table 4 indicates that adding CDs (1–3 % w/w) to the PSS film did not significantly alter the water contact angle, which ranged from 65.69° to 61.76°. This observation is consistent with findings from other studies using natural polymers, which reported similar water contact angle values ranging approximately from 58.27° to 62.43° [76] and 61.5° to 64.1° [13]. However, when CDs were incorporated into the PSS films at concentrations exceeding 3 % w/w, the water contact angle decreased to 48.6°. This decrease can be attributed to the hydrophilic properties of functional groups on the surface of CDs, which affect the film hydrophilicity [76]. Additionally, such decrease could have resulted from CDs disrupting the orderly arrangement of starch molecular chains, thereby reducing crystallinity and allowing greater water penetration into the film. The WVTR of the PSS-CD films showed a slight tendency to increase when CDs were added, although this change was not statistically significant. This slight increase in WVTR is likely due to the high hydrophilicity of the CDs. However, the incorporation of CDs into the PSS film at concentrations exceeding 3 % w/w resulted in a significant increase in WVTR, consistent with the water contact angle results.

During the initial hour of immersion, the water swelling of all PSS-CDs films exceeded that of the PSS film, with films containing higher CD content exhibiting higher swelling compared to those with lower CD content (Fig. 8 ). This indicates an increased hydrophilicity in the PSS-CDs films, likely due to the water solubility of CDs. This solubility enhances water uptake in PSS films upon CD incorporation by increasing the number of hydrophilic groups in the PSS-CDs. However, after 5 h of immersion, while the water swelling of the PSS-CDs3% film remained constant, that of the PSS film and the PSS-CDs6% film decreased. This change might be attributed to the release of some weakly interacting polymer chains from the film structure into the water medium, resulting in reduced water swelling, which aligns with the water solubility results (Table 4). These results indicate that the addition of CDs at 3%w/w decreased the swelling and solubility of the PSS film.

The thermal stability of the PSS-CDs films was assessed using thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), a method involving a heating process that breaks down bonds within the molecules of a material [78]. The TGA and derivative thermogravimetry (DTG) curves are shown in Fig. 9 .

The TGA thermograms of the PSS-CDs films with 0–6 % CDs revealed three primary stages of degradation. The initial mass loss occurred between 25 °C and 145 °C due to the evaporation of bound and free water in the films. The second thermal degradation phase started at 150 °C and ended at 250 °C, with the maximum degradation at 200 °C, attributed to the breakdown of glycerol and low-molecular-weight PSS. The third thermal degradation started at around 255 °C and ended at 380 °C, with a maximum of 320 °C, representing the decomposition of high-molecular-weight PSS (polysaccharides) [79]. The addition of a higher amounts of CDs resulted in a lower maximum decomposition temperature. However, the thermal stability behavior of the PSS-CDs films with 1–3 % w/w CDs was not significantly different, whereas the PSS-CDs6% films notably differed from the PSS film. The PSS-CDs6% film exhibited greater moisture loss compared to the other films, possibly due to the entrapment of water molecules by CDs [67]. The incorporation of CDs into the film resulted in a slight increase in the hydrophilicity of the biopolymer-based film. In general, the hydrodynamic behavior of a composite film is highly influenced by the type of polymer matrix and nanofiller used [14].

The thermal degradation behavior of PSS-CD films containing 1 % and 3 % w/w CDs revealed enhanced thermal stability in comparison to pure PSS films. This enhancement is primarily attributed to the formation of hydrogen bonds between the functional groups of CDs and the film matrix [67]. During the initial state of weight loss, the PSS-CD films with 1 % and 3 % w/w CDs exhibited a slower rate of mass reduction, likely due to moisture entrapment by the hydrophilic CDs. Consequently, the peak degradation temperature increased slightly relative to that of the pure PSS film. Similarly, in the third stage (the primary decomposition state) the PSS-CD films experienced a slower rate of degradation compared to the pure PSS film. This is potentially due to hydrogen bond interactions between the hydroxyl groups in PSS and the CDs, thereby improving the film's thermal stability. Fig. 9 (c-f) illustrates the weight loss patterns for each film. The residual mass at 600 °C for the PSS, PSS-CDs1%, PSS-CDs3%, and PSS-CDs6% films was 0 %, 10.7 %, 16.7 %, and 14.1 %, respectively, which is likely attributable to the presence of noncombustible minerals and impurities within the biopolymer matrix.

The FE-SEM images of the PSS and PSS-CDs films incorporated with various CDs concentrations are shown in Fig. 10 . The surface of the PSS film appeared slightly rough, curved, and compact, with no noticeable impurities, delamination, pores, or cracks observed (Fig. 10). Incorporating 3 % w/w CDs (PSS-CD3%) did not alter the surface morphology, as the film remained free from cracks or cavities, indicating compatibility between the synthesized CDs and the PSS matrix. However, some agglomerates were observed at 6 % w/w CDs (PSS-CD6%) possibly due to the excess CDs amount. The interaction of hydroxyl groups between CDs and PSS influenced the ordered arrangement of the polymer backbones due to the crosslinking process of the films [80]. The dispersion of CDs in the polymer matrix was likely facilitated by hydrogen-bonding interactions between the carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amine groups present on the surface of the CDs and the hydroxyl groups of PSS. Cross-sectional images revealed uniform dispersion of CDs in the polymer matrices, indicating good compatibility between the CDs and PSS.

The controlled release of antioxidants from packaging films is vital in maintaining efficacy and extending antioxidant activity. Antioxidant activity was studied in terms of the concentration required to yield a 50 % antioxidant activity (IC50). The IC50 of the PSS-CDs films were determined by DPPH radical scavenging activity, as shown in Table 5 .

The PSS film without CDs exhibited no antioxidant activity as it did not scavenge the DPPH radical (data not shown). However, films containing CDs displayed radical scavenging activity, which increased with higher CDs concentrations (Table 5). CDs can inactivate DPPH free radicals by transporting hydrogen, NH2 electrons, and combinations of both [81]. According to other studies, the addition of CDs to packaging films resulted in a strong antioxidant property and the production of active films [38,64,75,82]. The high antioxidant activity of CDs may be attributed to the presence of surface hydroxyl groups of CDs. The free radicals DPPH• can be reduced and quenched by H• transfer from the hydroxyl groups of the CDs. It is believed that the high antioxidant activity of CDs results from the presence of numerous electron-donating groups such as hydroxyl and carboxyl [83,84]. Nevertheless, CD incorporation significantly enhanced the free radical scavenging effect, resulting in films with superior antioxidant activity compared to the PSS film. Therefore, the obtained PSS-CDs films are deemed effective for active food packaging applications, extending shelf-life by retarding oxidation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of PSS-CD3% active films to pork preservation
ce:section.ce:para: The quality, nutritional value, and bioactivity of food, including odor, flavor, and color, are significantly affected by UV light [69]. UV-mediated spoilage often manifests first as deterioration in the color properties and appearance of food. To investigate the packaging effect of the PSS-CDs film, fresh pork was used to monitor changes in surface color during storage. A commercial PVC wrap film was also used as a reference to compare the properties of the prepared film. The typical UV-light transmission (%T) and water vapor transmission rate (WVTR) of commercial PVC wrap films are 15–40 g/m2·day (at 38 °C and 90 % RH) and 80–95 % in the 280–400 nm range, respectively [85,86].

Table 6 and Fig. 11 present the actual appearance of the test samples at various intervals of UV exposure during storage at 30 °C. Initially, the color of the test meat was uniform, reflecting its original red hue. However, after 3 h of storage under UV light, the control sample shifted from red to grayish with a rancid odor (Table 6). In contrast, pork samples covered with PSS-CDs films showed retarded color changes, odor, or other negative effects. Among the PSS-CDs films, the PSS-CDs3% film demonstrated the highest efficacy in preserving the redness of the meat samples, establishing itself as the most effective UV-barrier packaging film in the experiment. The observed color changes in the meat samples were further evaluated using color coordinates (L, a, b, and ∆E) determined by the Chroma meter.

Overall, the PSS-CDs3% film exhibited superior UV protection, complemented by excellent mechanical, antioxidant, and antibacterial properties, making it an effective choice for UV-blocking food packaging to safeguard packed food against photo-oxidation. Therefore, the PSS-CDs3% film was selected for testing under real storage conditions in a cold refrigerator at 12 ± 2 °C for up to 6 days. Following this storage period, the overall quality of the samples was evaluated through physicochemical analyses. Additionally, to assess the perception of positive (good) and negative (bad) odors in fresh pork samples, questionnaires were administered, with results presented in Table 7 .

The results in Table 7 indicate that the PSS-CDs3% film effectively preserved the red color of fresh pork during storage. The incorporation of CDs into the film contributed to the reduction of both oxidation and discoloration owing to their antioxidant activity. Furthermore, fresh pork packaged with the active PSS-CDs3% film exhibited the lowest odor score compared to unwrapped pork and other films. This can be attributed to the antimicrobial properties of CDs within the film. The active PSS-CDs3% film not only mitigated oxidation but also inhibited the growth of microorganisms, thus preserving the sensory characteristics of fresh pork. Consequently, this developed active film has the potential to enhance the safety of meat and meat products.

The effectiveness of PSS-CD3% active films in pork preservation was also evaluated through total volatile basic nitrogen (TVB-N) analysis (Table 8 ). The initial TVB-N value for all pork samples was 1.71 ± 0.07 mgN/100 g at day 0, confirming the freshness of the starting material. During the first three days of storage, all samples maintained stable TVB-N values with no statistically significant changes from the initial measurements (p > 0.05). By day 6, significant differences emerged among the packaging treatments. Pork samples packaged with PSS-CDs3% film exhibited remarkable stability, maintaining TVB-N values at 2.54 ± 0.35 mgN/100 g, which were not significantly different from their day 3 values (p > 0.05). However, unpackaged samples showed the highest TVB-N increase (12.44 ± 2.65 mgN/100 g), followed by commercial film-wrapped samples (4.62 ± 1.32 mgN/100 g) and PSS film-wrapped samples (3.36 ± 0.74 mgN/100 g), all showing significant increases from their respective day 3 values (p < 0.05).

The preservation efficacy of PSS-CDs3% films can be evaluated in the context of established freshness standards. According to previous studies, meat is considered fresh when TVB-N values remain below 15 mg/100 g or 10 mgN/100 g, while the SNI 2354.8:2009 standard sets acceptable consumption levels between 20 and 30 mgN/100 g [48,87]. The progressive increase in TVB-N values observed in control and other packaging treatments indicates protein degradation into volatile base compounds, a key indicator of quality deterioration. This degradation typically results from microbial activity, which produces various malodorous compounds including ammonia, skatole, indole compounds, mercaptans, hydrogen sulfide, amines, and cadaverine. The superior performance of PSS-CDs3% films can be attributed to the antimicrobial properties of incorporated CDs, which appear to inhibit both bacterial decomposition and autolytic enzyme activity, thereby reducing the formation of nitrogenous base compounds. These findings are consistent with previous research demonstrating the ability of CDs to suppress microbial enzymes responsible for protein degradation and volatile base compound formation, suggesting their potential as an effective preservative component in active packaging systems.

Spherical carbon dots (CDs) measuring 2.36 ± 0.33 nm in diameter were successfully synthesized from agricultural waste (pineapple leaves) using a hydrothermal method under optimized conditions. These CDs demonstrated antibacterial properties against both S. aureus and E. coli bacteria. Subsequently, the CDs were integrated into pineapple stem starch (PSS), also sourced from agricultural waste, to create multifunctional active films. The addition of CDs enhanced the physical and functional characteristics of the PSS film. Furthermore, the PSS-CDs films effectively blocked over 95 % of UV radiation and exhibited potent antioxidant activity. Among the PSS-CDs films, those containing 3 % w/w CDs displayed superior properties, including enhanced mechanical strength, reduced water vapor transmission rate (WVTR), water swelling, and solubility, while maintaining film integrity. Notably, during a 3 h storage period at 30 °C, the PSS-CDs3% film successfully preserved the color of packaged meat. Therefore, multifunctional PSS-CDs films developed in this study could be considered as a natural alternative for active food packaging applications to protect packaged products from UV-induced photooxidation and microbial contamination.
